name: Andrew Gold
description: Andrew Gold was a versatile multi-instrumentalist and songwriter who played a pivotal role in the Southern California soft rock scene. Before his solo breakthrough, he was a key member of Linda Ronstadt's band, contributing essential arrangements to her major 1970s hits. As a solo artist, he became known for his melodic pop sensibilities and polished production style.
key_songs_70s: Thank You for Being a Friend
trivia: Gold played most of the instruments on Linda Ronstadt's number one hit 'You're No Good.' He also served as a second engineer on part of Joni Mitchell's landmark album 'Blue.'

name: Anne Murray
description: Anne Murray was the first Canadian female solo singer to reach No. 1 on the U.S. charts, paving the way for future international success for Canadian artists. Her sound combined country roots with adult contemporary polish, characterized by her smooth alto voice. She remained a fixture on both pop and country charts throughout the 1970s with a string of wholesome, melodic hits.
key_songs_70s: I Just Fall in Love Again
trivia: Murray was a regular on the U.S. television series 'The Glen Campbell Goodtime Hour' during the early 70s. She became the first Canadian to earn a Gold record for her signature 1970 song 'Snowbird.'

name: Barry Manilow
description: Barry Manilow rose to prominence in the mid-1970s as a leading figure in adult contemporary and soft rock. Initially working as Bette Midler's pianist and producer, he transitioned to a solo career that yielded a massive string of dramatic, orchestral pop hits. His sound is defined by big piano ballads and catchy, upbeat anthems that dominated the Billboard charts throughout the late 1970s.
trivia: Manilow served as the musical director and producer for Bette Midler's first two albums in the early 1970s. His breakthrough hit 'Mandy' was the final number one hit for Bell Records before it was reorganized as Arista.

name: Billy Joel
description: Billy Joel emerged in the 1970s as one of the era's most successful singer-songwriters, blending pop, rock, and Broadway sensibilities. After early struggles, his breakthrough 'The Stranger' became a cultural phenomenon and established him as a master of narrative storytelling. His sound evolved from introspective piano ballads to sophisticated pop-rock, making him a fixture of FM radio and sold-out arenas.
trivia: For six months in the early 70s, Joel worked at a Los Angeles piano bar under the pseudonym 'Bill Martin,' an experience that inspired his signature song 'Piano Man.' His 1977 album 'The Stranger' surpassed Simon & Garfunkel's 'Bridge over Troubled Water' as Columbia's bestselling album at the time.

name: Billy Swan
description: Swan's solo career in the 1970s was defined by his massive crossover hit 'I Can Help,' which topped both pop and country charts. His sound integrated rockabilly energy with a laid-back Nashville production style that appealed to a broad audience. Despite the success of his debut, he remained a versatile industry figure, continuing to work as a session musician and producer throughout the decade.
key_songs_70s: Just Want to Taste Your Wine
trivia: The hit 'I Can Help' was written in under twenty minutes using an organ that was a wedding gift from Kris Kristofferson and Rita Coolidge.
Swan produced Tony Joe White's Top Ten hit 'Polk Salad Annie' before achieving his own solo stardom.
He participated in the historic 1979 Havana Jam festival alongside artists like Stephen Stills and Billy Joel.

name: Bonnie Raitt
description: Throughout the 1970s, Raitt established herself as a critically acclaimed interpreter of blues and folk-rock, notable for her bottleneck guitar skills. Her sound integrated elements of country and R&B, though commercial success remained modest for much of the decade. She achieved a major commercial breakthrough toward the end of the 1970s with a hit remake of 'Runaway.'
key_songs_70s: Give It Up or Let Me Go
Your Good Thing (Is About to End)
trivia: Raitt was a founding member of Musicians United for Safe Energy (MUSE) in 1979, helping to organize the 'No Nukes' concerts.
B.B. King once called her the best slide player working in popular music during her career.
Her version of 'Runaway' was recast as a heavy R&B track inspired by the rhythmic groove of Al Green.

name: Boz Scaggs
description: Scaggs transitioned from a blues-rock background into a polished blue-eyed soul and soft-rock sound that dominated the mid-to-late 1970s. His landmark 1976 album 'Silk Degrees' became a cultural phenomenon, showcasing a sophisticated urban production style. This sound made him a staple of FM radio and helped define the smooth 'West Coast' aesthetic of the era.
trivia: Scaggs is credited with helping the formation of Toto, as he hand-picked future members for his studio and touring band.
During a 1977 concert in New York, the power went out during the infamous July 13th blackout, forcing him to reschedule.
John Travolta choreographed his own dance to the song 'Lowdown' before the Bee Gees became involved with Saturday Night Fever.

name: Bread
description: Bread was a quintessential soft rock band that defined the 'mellow' sound of the early 1970s. Fronted by David Gates, the group achieved consistent success with highly melodic, acoustic-driven ballads that focused on themes of love and heartbreak. Their polished production and gentle vocal harmonies made them staples of the Adult Contemporary and Top 40 radio formats.
trivia: The group's name was inspired by a bread truck they saw while looking for band name ideas. Members Larry Knechtel and David Gates were highly sought-after session musicians before and after the band's success.

name: Captain & Tennille
description: Captain & Tennille were a husband-and-wife duo who became one of the most popular acts of the mid-to-late 1970s. Their sound blended pop, light rock, and middle-of-the-road influences, often featuring Daryl Dragon's intricate keyboard work and Toni Tennille's soulful vocals. They scored several massive hits and even hosted their own successful television variety series during the peak of their fame.
key_songs_70s: The Way I Want to Touch You
You Never Done It Like That
Do That to Me One More Time
trivia: The duo's first hit, 'Love Will Keep Us Together,' won the Grammy Award for Record of the Year in 1976. Toni Tennille famously sang backup vocals on the track 'Sedaka is back' as a tribute to the song's co-writer, Neil Sedaka.
youtube_search: Captain & Tennille Love Will Keep Us Together

name: Carly Simon
description: Carly Simon was one of the most popular confessional singer-songwriters of the early 1970s. Her music was known for its intelligent lyrics, distinctive contralto voice, and a sophisticated blend of folk, pop, and rock. Throughout the decade, she released a string of iconic hits that captured the romantic and social anxieties of her generation.
key_songs_70s: That's the Way I've Always Heard It Should Be
Haven't Got Time for the Pain
trivia: The song 'Anticipation' was written in just 15 minutes while Simon was waiting for a date with Cat Stevens. Her massive 1972 hit 'You're So Vain' was famously the subject of decades of speculation regarding its identity.

name: Carole King
description: After a legendary career as a Brill Building songwriter in the 1960s, Carole King transitioned to a solo performing career that defined the 1970s singer-songwriter era. Her 1971 album 'Tapestry' became a cultural landmark, staying on the charts for over six years and winning four Grammys. Her sound, rooted in piano-driven arrangements and warm, earnest vocals, provided a comforting soundtrack for a generation.
trivia: King wrote or co-wrote 118 songs that charted on the Billboard Hot 100 during the 20th century. Her album 'Tapestry' held the record for most weeks at number one by a female artist for over 20 years.

name: Cat Stevens
description: Cat Stevens was a leading voice of the early 1970s folk-rock movement, known for his spiritual lyrics and accessible, melodic songwriting. Following a recovery from tuberculosis, he moved toward a stripped-down, acoustic sound that resulted in several multi-platinum albums. His music often explored philosophical questions and social themes, delivered with a distinctive, emotive vocal style.
trivia: In 1971 and 1972, Stevens was romantically linked to Carly Simon, and both wrote songs about each other during their relationship. He sold the song 'The First Cut Is the Deepest' for just £30 in the 1960s before it became a hit for multiple artists.

name: Christopher Cross
description: Christopher Cross achieved sudden and massive success at the very end of the 1970s with his eponymous debut album. His sound epitomized the high-production values of late-decade soft rock, characterized by his clean guitar work and ethereal vocals. He famously swept the 'Big Four' Grammy categories in a single year, becoming a symbol of the peak of the soft rock era.
trivia: Cross is the only artist to win all four major Grammy Awards (Best New Artist, Record, Song, and Album of the Year) in a single ceremony for his 1979 debut. He is the son of a U.S. Army pediatrician who once treated President Eisenhower's grandchildren.

name: Dan Fogelberg
description: Dan Fogelberg was a multi-instrumentalist whose work combined folk, country, and pop into a smooth, melodic soft rock style. After moving to Nashville to hone his skills, he released a series of Gold and Platinum albums that showcased his gift for sensitive songwriting and lush arrangements. He became one of the defining voices of the late 1970s acoustic-rock movement.
trivia: Fogelberg was discovered by Irving Azoff and sent to Nashville to become a session musician before his solo career took off. He taught himself to play guitar using a Mel Bay course book and a Hawaiian slide guitar given to him by his grandfather.

name: David Gates
description: As the primary songwriter and frontman for Bread, Gates became one of the defining architects of the soft-rock sound in the early 1970s. His music was characterized by lush melodies and sentimental lyrics that consistently reached the top of the adult contemporary charts. Following Bread's dissolution, he maintained a successful solo career, including writing the hit theme for the film 'The Goodbye Girl.'
trivia: Gates formed his first high school band with a piano player who later became famous as Leon Russell.
He used his music royalties to purchase and operate a 1,400-acre cattle ranch in Northern California during the 1970s.
The song 'We've Only Just Begun,' which he co-produced, was originally written for a bank television commercial.

name: Don McLean
description: McLean became an international superstar in the early 1970s with the release of the epic 'American Pie,' which explored the shifting landscape of American culture. His sound blended traditional folk influences with pop sensibilities, resulting in a series of highly introspective and successful ballads. Throughout the decade, he remained a major concert attraction, balancing original compositions with classical pop standards.
trivia: The original working manuscript for 'American Pie' sold at auction in 2015 for over 1.2 million dollars.
McLean learned the art of performing while accompanying Pete Seeger on a boat trip up the Hudson River to raise environmental awareness.
Lori Lieberman's 'Killing Me Softly with His Song' was inspired by her reaction to seeing McLean perform in 1971.

name: Elton John
description: Elton John was arguably the most successful solo artist of the 1970s, achieving an unprecedented string of number-one albums and singles. His collaboration with lyricist Bernie Taupin yielded a vast catalogue of songs that ranged from piano-based soft rock to glam-infused stadium anthems. His melodic genius and flamboyant stage persona made him a dominant force in global popular music throughout the decade.

key_albums_70s.title: Don't Shoot Me I'm Only the Piano Player

trivia: John achieved a record-breaking streak of seven consecutive number-one albums in the United States between 1972 and 1975. As a teenager, he began wearing horn-rimmed glasses to imitate his idol, Buddy Holly.

name: Emmylou Harris
description: Harris was a pivotal figure in the 1970s country-rock movement, uniting rock and country audiences with her distinctive crystalline voice. Following her landmark collaboration with Gram Parsons, she launched a prolific solo career characterized by sophisticated arrangements and traditionalist roots. Her 1970s albums were both critical and commercial successes, featuring her legendary Hot Band and producing multiple number-one country hits.

key_albums_70s.title: Quarter Moon in a Ten Cent Town

key_songs_70s: If I Could Only Win Your Love
trivia: After Gram Parsons' death, Harris wrote 'Boulder to Birmingham' as a tribute to her mentor and musical partner.
During her high school years in Virginia, she was the class valedictorian and won the 'Miss Woodbridge' beauty pageant.
She provided prominent backing vocals for several tracks on Bob Dylan's 1975 album 'Desire.'

name: England Dan & John Ford Coley
description: England Dan & John Ford Coley were a Texas-based duo who became major stars of the soft rock movement in the late 1970s. Their sound was characterized by smooth vocal harmonies and melodic, acoustic-led pop arrangements. After struggle with early record deals, they found massive success with a string of Top 10 hits that epitomized the era's light and accessible radio style.

key_albums_70s.title: I'd Really Love to See You Tonight

key_songs_70s: I'd Really Love to See You Tonight
We'll Never Have to Say Goodbye Again
trivia: Dan Seals was the younger brother of Jim Seals from Seals & Crofts. The duo's signature hit 'I'd Really Love to See You Tonight' was heard through a wall by a record executive who immediately offered them a deal.
youtube_search: England Dan & John Ford Coley I'd Really Love to See You Tonight

name: Firefall
description: Firefall was a Colorado-based band that successfully blended country-rock with melodic soft rock. Formed by former members of several notable acts, they developed a polished sound that featured tight harmonies and clean, catchy arrangements. They achieved significant chart success in the late 1970s, becoming one of the most prominent groups in the West Coast pop-rock scene.
trivia: The band's biggest hit, 'You Are the Woman,' reached the Top 10 in 1976. Founder Rick Roberts had previously toured with Gram Parsons and the Fallen Angels before forming the group.

name: Gerry Rafferty
description: Gerry Rafferty was a Scottish singer-songwriter who achieved solo stardom in the late 1970s after leaving the band Stealers Wheel. His sound combined folk roots with highly sophisticated, jazz-inflected production and introspective lyrics often exploring themes of alienation. His landmark 1978 album 'City to City' features one of the era's most iconic saxophone-led arrangements.

key_albums_70s.title: Can I Have My Money Back?

key_songs_70s: Stuck in the Middle with You
trivia: The iconic saxophone riff in 'Baker Street' was originally composed by Rafferty as an electric guitar solo on his demo. Rafferty consistently refused to tour the United States despite his massive success there.

name: Gilbert O'Sullivan
description: Gilbert O'Sullivan was an Irish singer-songwriter who achieved major international success in the early 1970s. Known for his percussive piano style and witty, observational lyrics, he initially stood out with a unique visual image inspired by silent film stars. His sound blended traditional pop with contemporary soft rock, yielding several multi-million selling singles.

key_albums_70s.title: I'm a Writer, Not a Fighter
A Stranger in My Own Back Yard

trivia: O'Sullivan initially performed with a 'depression-era street urchin' look, complete with bowl cut and short trousers. His disc sales in 1972 exceeded 10 million, making him the top star of that year.

name: Glen Campbell
description: Glen Campbell was a revered session guitarist and 'Wrecking Crew' veteran who became a major crossover star in the late 1960s and 1970s. His sound successfully bridged country, pop, and soft rock, often featuring his incredible guitar work and smooth vocal delivery. Throughout the 1970s, he remained a fixture on television and the charts, releasing some of the era's most enduring melodic hits.

key_albums_70s.title: The Last Time I Saw Her
I Knew Jesus (Before He Was a Star)

key_songs_70s: The Last Time I Saw Her
Country Boy (You Got Your Feet in L.A.)
trivia: Campbell was a prominent member of 'The Wrecking Crew' and played on hundreds of hits for artists like the Beach Boys and Frank Sinatra. He famously befriended Elvis Presley while recording a soundtrack in 1964, noting they were both raised 'picking cotton.'

name: Gordon Lightfoot
description: Gordon Lightfoot was a Canadian singer-songwriter who helped define the introspective folk-rock sound of the 1970s. His songs, often centered on themes of nature, travel, and loneliness, are characterized by his rich baritone and masterful acoustic guitar work. He achieved worldwide success with a series of polished, narrative-driven hits that became timeless soft rock staples.
key_songs_70s: If You Could Read My Mind
The Wreck of the Edmund Fitzgerald
trivia: Lightfoot was recognized on a limited edition Canada Post stamp in 2007 alongside Joni Mitchell. His signature 1970 hit 'If You Could Read My Mind' sold over a million copies and marked the major turning point in his international career.

name: Harry Chapin
description: Harry Chapin was a singer-songwriter best known for his vivid, novelistic 'story songs' that often dealt with social issues and personal relationships. His sound blended folk-rock and pop with orchestral elements, supporting his theatrical delivery. He achieved immense popularity in the 1970s, using his platform to become a leading activist against world hunger.

key_albums_70s.title: On the Road to Kingdom Come

key_songs_70s: I Wanna Learn a Love Song
trivia: Chapin was the first performer in the history of 'The Tonight Show' to be called back to perform the next night after his appearance singing 'Taxi.' His song 'W.O.L.D.' was inspired by listening to a radio DJ calling his ex-wife from a studio.
youtube_search: Harry Chapin Cat's in the Cradle

name: Helen Reddy
description: Helen Reddy was an Australian-American singer who became the 'Queen of '70s Pop' with a string of massive hits on the Billboard charts. Her sound was defined by sophisticated adult contemporary arrangements and empowering lyrics, most notably her signature anthem 'I Am Woman.' She was a dominant force on both pop and easy listening radio throughout the mid-1970s.

key_albums_70s.title: I Don't Know How to Love Him
No Way to Treat a Lady

key_songs_70s: I Don't Know How to Love Him
You and Me Against the World
Ain't No Way to Treat a Lady
trivia: Reddy was the first Australian to host a weekly one-hour primetime variety show on an American network. She famously encouraged her friend Olivia Newton-John to move to the United States to pursue her career.

name: Jackson Browne
description: Jackson Browne was a central figure in the Southern California singer-songwriter community, initially gaining fame for writing songs for other artists. His own solo work in the 1970s was characterized by highly literate, introspective lyrics and melodic rock arrangements. His signature 1977 album 'Running on Empty' broke conventions by being a live concept album about life on the road.
trivia: In 1979, Browne co-founded the organization Musicians United for Safe Energy (MUSE) following the Three Mile Island nuclear accident. Bruce Springsteen once told him, 'You wrote the songs they [the Eagles] wished they had written.'

name: James Taylor
description: James Taylor achieved a massive breakthrough in 1970, becoming a leading figure in the gentle, introspective singer-songwriter movement. His sound was defined by his intricate finger-picking guitar style and smooth, comforting vocals that often dealt with personal struggles and emotional recovery. He became a fixture of the 1970s music scene, consistently delivering Platinum-selling albums and enduring folk-rock classics.

key_albums_70s.title: Mud Slide Slim and the Blue Horizon

key_songs_70s: Don't Let Me Be Lonely Tonight
How Sweet It Is (To Be Loved by You)
trivia: Taylor's 1976 'Greatest Hits' album was certified Diamond and remains one of the best-selling albums in US history. He famously played the leading role in the 1971 cult film 'Two-Lane Blacktop.'

name: Janis Ian
description: Janis Ian was a folk-pop prodigy who first achieved notoriety as a teenager in the 1960s before reaching her commercial peak in the mid-1970s. Her music was marked by its intense lyrical honesty and sophisticated, jazz-inflected folk arrangements. Her 1975 masterpiece 'At Seventeen' remains a definitive anthem of adolescent angst and social alienation.
trivia: Ian was the second musical guest on the series premiere of 'Saturday Night Live' in 1975. On Valentine's Day 1977, she received 461 cards from fans after stating in her song 'At Seventeen' that she never received one as a teen.

name: Jim Croce
description: Jim Croce was a master storyteller of the early 1970s folk-rock scene, known for his warm baritone and songs about everyday working-class characters. After years of odd jobs, he achieved massive success through his partnership with guitarist Maury Muehleisen. His career was tragically cut short at its peak, but his melodic ballads and narrative hits remained dominant on the charts throughout the decade.

key_albums_70s.title: You Don't Mess Around with Jim

key_songs_70s: You Don't Mess Around with Jim
Operator (That's Not the Way It Feels)
I'll Have to Say I Love You in a Song
Workin' at the Car Wash Blues
trivia: Croce finished recording his final album 'I Got a Name' just one week before his tragic death in a plane crash. His song 'Time in a Bottle' became a massive posthumous number one hit due to its resonant lyrics about mortality.
youtube_search: Jim Croce Time in a Bottle

name: Jim Stafford
description: Stafford achieved prominence in the mid-1970s with a series of novelty-tinged hits that blended clever lyrics with folk-pop melodies. His sound was distinct for its humor and storytelling, often focusing on offbeat characters and southern imagery. In addition to his recording success, he became a major television personality during the decade, hosting his own variety series on ABC.
key_songs_70s: I Got Stoned and I Missed It
trivia: Stafford's biggest hit, 'Spiders & Snakes,' was a million-seller that reached number three on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1974.
In high school, he played in a band called the Legends along with future stars Lobo and Gram Parsons.
He was married to singer-songwriter Bobbie Gentry for two years in the late 1970s.

name: John Denver
description: John Denver was one of the most beloved and best-selling entertainers of the 1970s, characterized by his wholesome image and songs celebrating nature and simple living. His sound blended folk, country, and acoustic pop into a series of massive crossover hits that dominated global charts. He became a leading advocate for environmental and humanitarian causes while maintaining a heavy presence on television and film.
key_songs_70s: Thank God I'm a Country Boy
trivia: Denver wrote his signature hit 'Annie's Song' in just ten minutes while sitting on a ski lift in Aspen, Colorado. His manager Jerry Weintraub once remarked that he had to 'get him to the people' because the critics were often resistant to his wholesome style.

name: John Prine
description: John Prine was a seminal figure in the Chicago folk scene, achieving national attention in the early 1970s for his humorous, insightful, and often satirical songwriting. His debut album is widely regarded as a masterpiece, featuring songs that explored the human condition with a unique blend of empathy and wit. Throughout the decade, he maintained a loyal following with his stripped-down, country-inflected folk-rock sound.
trivia: Prine worked as a mailman in Chicago while writing many of the songs for his acclaimed debut album. Kris Kristofferson famously remarked that Prine wrote songs so good 'we'll have to break his thumbs.'

name: John Sebastian
description: After departing the Lovin' Spoonful, Sebastian launched a solo career in the 1970s that maintained his reputation as a gentle and positive songwriter. His sound during this era was grounded in folk and blues, often featuring his signature harmonica work and tie-dyed aesthetic. He achieved a major commercial resurgence in the mid-1970s with the chart-topping theme for the sitcom 'Welcome Back, Kotter.'

key_albums_70s.title: Cheapo Cheapo Productions Presents Real Live John Sebastian

trivia: Sebastian's unplanned, solo acoustic performance at Woodstock became a highlight of the festival documentary.
He is credited with helping to popularize the art of tie-dyeing clothing among music fans in the late 1960s and 1970s.
The song 'Welcome Back' reached number one on the Billboard charts in 1976.

name: Joni Mitchell
description: Joni Mitchell was a transformative figure of the 1970s, evolving from a leading folk artist to a highly experimental jazz-rock pioneer. Her 1971 album 'Blue' remains one of the most celebrated confessional records ever made, defining the singer-songwriter movement's emotional depth. Her work was characterized by unconventional guitar tunings, complex piano arrangements, and fiercely poetic lyrics that captured the nuances of personal relationships and fame.
trivia: Mitchell produced or co-produced almost all of her own albums and personally designed many of her own album covers. She famously noted that during the recording of 'Blue,' she felt like a 'cellophane wrapper on a pack of cigarettes' due to her total lack of emotional defenses.

name: Karla Bonoff
description: Bonoff was a central figure in the Southern California singer-songwriter scene, first gaining fame through other artists' recordings of her songs. Her 1970s sound epitomized the melodic and emotionally direct 'West Coast' soft-rock aesthetic. Signing as a solo artist in 1977, she released two influential albums that featured contributions from many of the era's top session musicians.
key_songs_70s: Someone To Lay Down Beside Me
When You Walk in the Room
trivia: Linda Ronstadt helped spark Bonoff's career by recording three of her songs for the 1976 album 'Hasten Down the Wind.'
By the age of 16, Bonoff and her sister had already recorded an 11-song demo for Elektra Records supervised by Bruce Botnick.
She was a founding member of the early songwriter collective Bryndle along with Andrew Gold and Wendy Waldman.
youtube_search: Karla Bonoff Someone To Lay Down Beside Me

name: Kenny Rogers
description: Kenny Rogers successfully transitioned from psychedelic rock in the late 1960s to become a dominant country-pop crossover star of the 1970s. His sound was defined by his smooth 'teddy-bear baritone' and narrative storytelling style. After his solo breakthrough with 'Lucille,' he released a string of massive hits that made him one of the most recognizable voices on both pop and country radio.
key_songs_70s: Ruby, Don't Take Your Love to Town
trivia: Rogers credited his duets with close friend Dottie West in the late 1970s for further cementing his career with country music audiences. His signature hit 'The Gambler' was later preserved in the National Recording Registry by the Library of Congress.

name: Kris Kristofferson
description: Kristofferson was a revolutionary force in 1970s country music, introducing a raw and literary songwriting style that defined the 'outlaw' movement. His sound moved away from polished Nashville production toward a more introspective and gritty folk-influenced approach. Throughout the decade, he successfully balanced a prolific music career with a high-profile presence as a leading man in Hollywood films.

key_albums_70s.title: The Silver Tongued Devil and I

key_songs_70s: Help Me Make It Through the Night
trivia: Before his music career, Kristofferson was a Rhodes Scholar and served as a captain and helicopter pilot in the U.S. Army.
He famously landed a helicopter on Johnny Cash's property to deliver demo tapes and get the star's attention.
His song 'Me and Bobby McGee' became a posthumous number-one hit for Janis Joplin.
youtube_search: Kris Kristofferson Help Me Make It Through the Night

name: Leo Sayer
description: Leo Sayer achieved major chart success in the mid-to-late 1970s with a versatile sound that moved between music hall influences and polished disco-pop. His career was launched under the management of Adam Faith and he quickly became a fixture of both UK and US charts. His sound was characterized by his energetic vocal delivery and melodic sensibilities that appealed to a broad international audience.
key_songs_70s: You Make Me Feel Like Dancing
trivia: Sayer famously performed his early hit 'The Show Must Go On' while wearing a pierrot clown costume and makeup. His disco-styled hit 'You Make Me Feel Like Dancing' won a Grammy Award for Best Rhythm and Blues Song.
youtube_search: Leo Sayer You Make Me Feel Like Dancing

name: Linda Ronstadt
description: Linda Ronstadt was the defining female rock star of the 1970s, celebrated for her incredible vocal range and her ability to master multiple genres. After early success with the Stone Poneys, she became the first female 'arena class' star, setting touring records and releasing a string of multi-platinum albums. Her sound blended country, rock, and soul, often popularizing songs by contemporary writers like Jackson Browne and Warren Zevon.
trivia: Ronstadt's first backing band in 1971 included Don Henley and Glenn Frey, who went on to form the Eagles. In 1977, she became the first female artist to have two songs in the Billboard Hot 100 Top Ten at the same time.

name: Lobo
description: Recording under the stage name Lobo, Roland Kent LaVoie achieved massive success in the 1970s with a melodic and gentle folk-pop sound. His music was characterized by its simplicity and recurring natural themes, making him a dominant force on the Adult Contemporary charts. Throughout the early and mid-1970s, he produced a consistent string of top-ten hits that became soft-rock staples.
key_songs_70s: Me and You and a Dog Named Boo
I'd Love You to Want Me
Don't Expect Me to Be Your Friend
It Sure Took a Long, Long Time
Where Were You When I Was Falling in Love
Standing at the End of the Line
trivia: The name 'Lobo' is Spanish for wolf, an alias he adopted in 1971 to let the music stand on its own.
His biggest hit, 'I'd Love You to Want Me,' was a million-seller that reached number one in Germany.
He began his musical career in a band called The Rumours alongside future stars Jim Stafford and Gram Parsons.
youtube_search: Lobo I'd Love You to Want Me

name: Loggins and Messina
description: Loggins and Messina formed accidentally when veteran producer Jim Messina intended to help introduce then-unknown songwriter Kenny Loggins to his audience. Their harmonies and instrumental chemistry were so successful that they became one of the leading musical duos of the early-to-mid 1970s. Their sound blended folk, rock, and light pop elements, resulting in several platinum albums and a heavy presence on FM radio.
trivia: Clive Davis personally intervened to convince the pair to record as a duo after seeing the excitement over their early recording sessions. The duo amicably parted in 1976 after selling more than 16 million records.
youtube_search: Loggins and Messina Your Mama Don't Dance

name: Loudon Wainwright III
description: Wainwright emerged in the 1970s as a witty and self-mocking singer-songwriter, often categorized alongside the 'new Dylans' of the era. His sound was largely acoustic and folk-based, known for its sharp humor and brutally honest autobiographical lyrics. He achieved a major crossover success with the novelty hit 'Dead Skunk' and appeared as the 'singing surgeon' on the series MASH.
trivia: In the early 1970s, he was one of many young folk singers tagged by the press as the 'new Dylan.'
His 1972 novelty song 'Dead Skunk (in the Middle of the Road)' remains his best-known commercial hit.
He appeared as Captain Calvin Spalding in three episodes of the television series MASH.

name: Mac Davis
description: Mac Davis first achieved fame as a songwriter, providing several major hits for Elvis Presley before launching a highly successful solo career. His sound epitomized the country-pop crossover style of the 1970s, characterized by his smooth delivery and relatable, often sentimental lyrics. He became a major multi-media star, hosting his own variety show and appearing in several feature films during the peak of his popularity.

key_albums_70s.title: Baby Don't Get Hooked on Me

key_songs_70s: Baby Don't Get Hooked on Me
Rock 'N' Roll (I Gave You the Best Years of My Life)
trivia: Davis wrote several massive hits for Elvis Presley, including 'In the Ghetto' and 'Memories.' His song 'Rock 'N' Roll (I Gave You the Best Years of My Life)' was famously the last song played by Los Angeles station KHJ before it switched formats in 1980.
youtube_search: Mac Davis Baby Don't Get Hooked on Me

name: Maria Muldaur
description: Muldaur transitioned from the Greenwich Village folk revival into solo stardom in the early 1970s with a sound that blended folk, jazz, and blues. Her hit single 'Midnight at the Oasis' became an iconic staple of the era, showcasing her distinctive vocal style and sultry arrangements. Throughout the decade, she remained a respected figure in roots music, collaborating with many prominent rock and bluegrass musicians.
trivia: Her version of the Peggy Lee standard 'I'm a Woman' reached number 12 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1974.
Muldaur served as a backing vocalist for the Jerry Garcia Band in the late 1970s.
She began her career as Maria D'Amato, performing in the Even Dozen Jug Band and Jim Kweskin & the Jug Band.
youtube_search: Maria Muldaur Midnight at the Oasis

name: Melanie
description: Melanie Anne Safka achieved international fame following her emotional performance at the 1969 Woodstock festival. Her sound combined raw, powerful vocals with simple folk-pop melodies, often centering on themes of peace and personal experience. She remained a unique and successful figure throughout the early 1970s, eventually forming her own independent record label to maintain artistic control.
key_songs_70s: What Have They Done to My Song Ma
trivia: Melanie was the first female performer to have three top 40 hits at the same time in the early 1970s. Her signature hit 'Brand New Key' was inspired by a desire to get a record deal and stand out with a catchy, novelty-inflected sound.

name: Neil Diamond
description: Neil Diamond emerged as one of the most successful pop-rock artists of the 1970s after a prolific career as a Brill Building songwriter. His sound mellowed in the early 70s as he moved to Los Angeles, resulting in a string of massive melodic hits that defined the adult contemporary radio sound. Known for his powerful vocals and charismatic stage presence, he became a dominant force in arena rock and cinematic pop.
key_songs_70s: If You Know What I Mean
trivia: Diamond wrote his signature hit 'Sweet Caroline' in just one hour while staying in a Memphis hotel. He famously began wearing colorful beaded shirts in concert so that audience members in the back could see him without binoculars.

name: Nicolette Larson
description: Larson rose to fame in the late 1970s as a frequent vocal accompanist for Neil Young before launching a successful solo career. Her 1978 debut album showcased a smooth blend of rock, country, and R&B that earned her immediate critical acclaim. Her hit cover of Neil Young's 'Lotta Love' became a defining soft-rock anthem of the late decade, reaching the top of the adult contemporary charts.
key_songs_70s: I Only Want to Be with You
trivia: Larson was named Female Vocalist of 1978 by Rolling Stone magazine shortly after her debut album was released.
She and Linda Ronstadt were billed as 'the Bullets' when they provided backing vocals for Neil Young's 'American Stars 'n Bars' album.
She appeared at the 1979 'No Nukes' concerts at Madison Square Garden, backed by the Doobie Brothers.

name: Olivia Newton-John
description: Olivia Newton-John was a British-Australian singer who became an international superstar in the 1970s. Initially known for her country-flavored pop ballads, her sound evolved through the decade toward more sophisticated soft rock and pop. Her starring role in the 1978 film 'Grease' catapulted her to a new level of fame, making her one of the highest-selling female artists of the era.

key_albums_70s.title: If You Love Me, Let Me Know

key_songs_70s: You're the One That I Want
trivia: Newton-John was the first British singer to win the CMA Female Vocalist of the Year award, a victory that initially sparked controversy among Nashville purists. She insisted on a screen test with John Travolta for 'Grease' to ensure they had sufficient chemistry.
youtube_search: Olivia Newton-John Hopelessly Devoted to You

name: Orleans
description: Orleans was a Woodstock-based band known for their intricate vocal harmonies and a sound that blended elements of rhythm and blues with melodic soft rock. While initially a high-energy live act, they achieved massive mainstream popularity with several polished pop-rock hits in the mid-to-late 1970s. Their music was noted for its clean guitar work and breezy, radio-friendly arrangements.
trivia: Rolling Stone once called Orleans 'the best unrecorded band in America' before they signed their first deal. Their hit 'Still the One' was famously chosen as the theme song for the ABC television network in 1977.

name: Paul Simon
description: Following the dissolution of Simon & Garfunkel, Paul Simon launched a highly successful solo career in the 1970s, exploring diverse genres including gospel, reggae, and soul. His sound remained rooted in melodic acoustic guitar but grew more rhythmically adventurous and lyrically complex. He released three landmark studio albums during the decade that cemented his status as a master of sophisticated pop-rock.

key_albums_70s.title: Still Crazy After All These Years

key_songs_70s: Me and Julio Down by the Schoolyard
Still Crazy After All These Years
trivia: Simon made his acting debut in Woody Allen's 1977 romantic comedy 'Annie Hall,' playing music producer Tony Lacey. His 1975 album 'Still Crazy After All These Years' won the Grammy for Album of the Year.
youtube_search: Paul Simon 50 Ways to Leave Your Lover

name: Paul Williams
description: Williams was one of the most successful and prolific songwriters of the 1970s, penning iconic soft-rock hits for acts like the Carpenters and Three Dog Night. His solo sound featured melodic pop and folk sensibilities, delivered with a distinctive and emotional vocal style. He also achieved significant fame as a film composer and actor, winning multiple awards for his contributions to cinema and television soundtracks.

key_albums_70s.title: Just an Old Fashioned Love Song
A Little on the Windy Side

key_songs_70s: I Won't Last a Day Without You
You and Me Against the World
trivia: Williams won an Academy Award and a Grammy for writing the lyrics to 'Evergreen,' the love theme from 'A Star Is Born.'
He auditioned unsuccessfully for the Monkees but later wrote the song 'Someday Man' for the group.
He starred as the villainous record producer Swan in the 1974 cult film Phantom of the Paradise.
youtube_search: Paul Williams An Old Fashioned Love Song

name: Player
description: Player was a British-American band that epitomized the late-1970s Los Angeles soft rock sound. Initially characterized by a 'blue-eyed soul' influence, they achieved massive success with the number one hit 'Baby Come Back.' Their sound later shifted toward harder rock after touring with Eric Clapton, but they are most remembered for their smooth, melodic pop sensibilities and polished production.
key_songs_70s: This Time I'm in It for Love
Love Is Where You Find It
trivia: Player was named Billboard's Best New Singles Artist of 1978. Lead singer Peter Beckett later joined the Little River Band and worked extensively as a film and television composer.

name: Poco
description: Formed from the ashes of Buffalo Springfield, Poco was a pioneering force in the 1970s country-rock scene, known for their vocal harmonies and melodic instrumentation. Throughout the decade, the band's lineup shifted frequently, featuring future members of the Eagles and Loggins & Messina. They eventually achieved their greatest commercial success in 1978 with a shift toward a polished soft-rock sound on the album 'Legend.'
trivia: Poco was originally named 'Pogo' after a comic strip character, but changed the name after the creator threatened to sue.
Their 1978 album 'Legend' featured cover art by graphic artist and future comedy actor Phil Hartman.
Future Eagles members Randy Meisner and Timothy B. Schmit both played bass in Poco during the 1970s.

name: Pure Prairie League
description: Pure Prairie League became a staple of 1970s country-rock, balancing acoustic sensibilities with electric energy and sophisticated vocal arrangements. Their sound gained massive traction through heavy touring on college campuses, eventually leading to the delayed massive success of the single 'Amie.' Throughout the decade, the band maintained a string of successful albums, featuring a fluid lineup of notable musicians.
key_songs_70s: I'll Fix Your Flat Tire, Merle
Falling In and Out of Love
trivia: The band's name was taken from a fictional temperance union in the 1939 Errol Flynn film 'Dodge City.'
Every Pure Prairie League album cover features a trail-worn cowboy named Sad Luke, based on a Norman Rockwell painting.
Future country superstar Vince Gill joined the band as a vocalist and multi-instrumentalist in 1978.

name: Randy Newman
description: In the 1970s, Newman stood apart from his contemporaries with a sound defined by orchestral arrangements and satirical lyrics delivered from the perspective of unreliable narrators. His music blended Americana, ragtime, and piano-pop, often tackling complex social and political themes with mordant wit. While achieving critical acclaim as a 'writer's writer,' he also scored significant commercial success with controversial hits toward the end of the decade.
key_songs_70s: Mama Told Me Not to Come
You Can Leave Your Hat On
trivia: Newman comes from an extended family of prominent Hollywood composers, including three uncles who were Oscar winners.
His 1977 hit 'Short People' became a subject of significant national controversy despite Newman intending it as a joke about prejudice.
His song 'Louisiana 1927' became a prominent cultural anthem in the aftermath of Hurricane Katrina.

name: Richie Havens
description: Havens was a powerful and soulful figure in the 1970s folk-rock scene, known for his rhythmic, open-tuned guitar style and raspy vocals. Following his iconic appearance at Woodstock, his sound integrated elements of rhythm and blues into his interpretations of pop and folk standards. Throughout the decade, he maintained a prolific recording schedule and became a frequent guest on educational television programs.
trivia: Havens concluded his iconic Woodstock set by improvising the song 'Freedom' based on the old spiritual 'Motherless Child.'
He was of Native American Blackfoot descent on his father's side and his grandfather had performed in Buffalo Bill's Wild West Show.
Havens was the voice of the popular GeoSafari educational toys in the late 20th century.

name: Roberta Flack
description: Roberta Flack was a defining voice of 1970s popular music, known for her emotive, genre-blending ballads that spanned R&B, jazz, folk, and pop. Her sophisticated arrangements and emotive delivery helped birth the 'quiet storm' radio format. She achieved historic success by winning the Grammy for Record of the Year in consecutive years for two of her most iconic hits.
key_songs_70s: The First Time Ever I Saw Your Face
Killing Me Softly with His Song
The Closer I Get to You
If Ever I See You Again
trivia: Flack was the first artist to win the Grammy Award for Record of the Year in consecutive years. For 40 years, she had an apartment in The Dakota building in New York City right next door to John Lennon and Yoko Ono.
youtube_search: Roberta Flack Killing Me Softly with His Song

name: Rupert Holmes
description: Rupert Holmes was a versatile musician, producer, and songwriter known for his witty 'story songs' and sophisticated pop production. After years as a successful session player and producer for artists like Barbra Streisand, he achieved massive solo success at the end of the 1970s. His work was characterized by clever lyrics, lush orchestration, and catchy, melodic pop sensibilities.
key_songs_70s: I Don't Want to Hold Your Hand
trivia: Holmes' landmark hit 'Escape (The Piña Colada Song)' was the final Hot 100 number one of 1979. He intentionally drew controversy with his early song 'Timothy,' which was about human cannibalism.
youtube_search: Rupert Holmes Escape (The Piña Colada Song)

name: Seals & Crofts
description: Seals & Crofts were one of the most successful soft rock duos of the 1970s, known for their warm harmonies and acoustic guitar-and-mandolin sound. Their music was characterized by positive, often spiritual lyrics influenced by their Baháʼí faith. They achieved consistent success with highly melodic hits that epitomized the breezy, sophisticated radio style of the era.
key_songs_70s: We May Never Pass This Way (Again)
trivia: Both Jim Seals and Dash Crofts were members of the Champs after the group's hit 'Tequila' reached number one in 1958. They performed at the massive California Jam festival in 1974 for over 200,000 fans.

name: Stephen Bishop
description: Stephen Bishop achieved success in the late 1970s as a purveyor of sensitive, acoustic-based soft rock. After years of rejection, his break came through the patronage of Art Garfunkel, who recorded several of his songs. Bishop's sound was defined by his smooth, high tenor and melodic songwriting, making him a fixture of the sophisticated adult contemporary scene.
key_songs_70s: The Same Old Tears on a New Background
Save It for a Rainy Day
trivia: Art Garfunkel, Eric Clapton, and Chaka Khan all contributed their talents to Bishop's 1976 debut album 'Careless.' Before his solo success, he spent a lean eight-year period being rejected by nearly every label in Los Angeles.

name: Terry Jacks
description: Jacks achieved worldwide stardom in 1974 with the release of 'Seasons in the Sun,' an adaptation of a Jacques Brel song that became one of the decade's biggest-selling singles. His sound was characterized by gentle, acoustic-based production and sentimental lyrical themes. Despite his massive success as a performer, he largely withdrew from the limelight mid-decade to focus on record production and environmental activism.
key_songs_70s: Rock 'N' Roll (I Gave You the Best Years of My Life)
trivia: 'Seasons in the Sun' was originally intended for the Beach Boys, but Jacks recorded it himself after they decided not to release it.
The song became the largest-selling international single by a Canadian artist at that time, selling 14 million copies.
In the late 1970s, Jacks was impersonated by a man who gave concerts and appeared on radio before turning himself in.
youtube_search: Terry Jacks Seasons in the Sun

name: The Carpenters
description: The Carpenters were a sibling duo whose polished, sophisticated sound epitomized soft rock and adult contemporary during the 1970s. Characterized by Richard's melodic arrangements and Karen's warm, distinctive contralto, they achieved immense global popularity. Their music, focused on themes of love and melancholy, resulted in a long string of number one hits and top-selling albums throughout the decade.
key_songs_70s: (They Long to Be) Close to You
trivia: Karen Carpenter was a proficient drummer and initially viewed herself as a 'drummer who sang.' The duo won the Grammy Award for Best New Artist in 1971, beating out artists like Elton John.

name: Tim Hardin
description: Hardin was a highly influential but commercially enigmatic figure in the early 1970s, recognized for his poetic and jazz-inflected folk-pop sound. His music often explored themes of addiction and heartbreak, delivered in a voice praised for its emotional slip-and-slide quality. While his own 1970s recordings found limited commercial reach due to personal struggles, his songs became major hits for many other artists of the era.
key_songs_70s: How Can We Hang On to a Dream
trivia: Bob Dylan reportedly called Hardin the 'greatest living songwriter' after hearing his first album in the late 1960s.
Hardin claimed to be a distant relative of the 19th-century outlaw John Wesley Hardin.
He performed at the 1969 Woodstock Festival, though his set was excluded from the original documentary and soundtrack album.

name: Tom Waits
description: In the 1970s, Waits developed a distinctive persona as a gritty bard of the streets, known for his gravelly voice and lyrics focusing on society's underbelly. His sound during this era blended folk, jazz, and blues, often centering on piano ballads and beat-influenced storytelling. He toured extensively during the decade, building an international cult following for his theatrical performances and character sketches.
key_songs_70s: Christmas Card from a Hooker in Minneapolis
I Hope That I Don't Fall in Love With You
trivia: Waits's unique voice has been described as sounding like it was 'soaked in a vat of bourbon' and then 'run over with a car.'
Before his music career, he worked at a pizza restaurant where he developed an interest in the lives and dialogue of the patrons.
He lived at the establishment West Hollywood Tropicana Motel during his rise to fame in the late 1970s.

name: Tony Orlando and Dawn
description: Tony Orlando and Dawn were one of the most successful pop-rock groups of the early-to-mid 1970s. Their sound combined catchy pop melodies with light rock and rhythm and blues influences, delivered with high-energy performances. They achieved several massive hits and transitioned into multi-media stars with their own popular television variety show.
key_songs_70s: Tie a Yellow Ribbon Round the Ole Oak Tree
Say, Has Anybody Seen My Sweet Gypsy Rose
He Don't Love You (Like I Love You)
trivia: Tony Orlando was a successful music executive before forming Dawn, hiring his future backing vocalists Telma Hopkins and Joyce Vincent Wilson while he was producing Barry Manilow. Their 1973 hit 'Tie a Yellow Ribbon Round the Ole Oak Tree' became a massive cultural phenomenon and one of the best-selling singles of the decade.
youtube_search: Tony Orlando and Dawn Tie a Yellow Ribbon Round the Ole Oak Tree

name: Van Morrison
description: Van Morrison achieved critical and commercial dominance in the 1970s with a unique sound termed 'Celtic soul.' His work combined rhythm and blues with jazz, folk, and spiritual themes, often delivered with a stream-of-consciousness lyrical style. He released a series of highly acclaimed studio and live albums during the decade that established him as one of the most influential recording artists of his era.

key_albums_70s.title: His Band and the Street Choir

trivia: Morrison's father had one of the largest record collections in Northern Ireland, providing the young Van with a deep education in blues and jazz. His 1971 album 'Tupelo Honey' famously features his then-wife Janet Planet on the cover.

name: Warren Zevon
description: Warren Zevon was a central figure in the Southern California rock scene, known for his acerbic wit and hard-boiled, often macabre narratives. After early work as a session player and songwriter for others, he achieved breakthrough solo success with the promotion of Jackson Browne. His sound blended literate folk-rock with a tough, cynical edge, delivering several of the era's most memorable and darkly humorous hits.
trivia: Zevon spent the early 1970s touring as the band leader and musical coordinator for the Everly Brothers. He famously composed 'Roland the Headless Thompson Gunner' while playing in a small tavern in Spain owned by a mercenary.

name: Wendy Waldman
description: Waldman was a highly respected member of the Los Angeles singer-songwriter community, known for her sophisticated compositions and production skills. Her 1970s sound integrated rock, pop, and country influences, often featuring contributions from many of her fellow California-based contemporaries. While her solo albums earned significant critical acclaim, she also became widely recognized for writing songs that became hits for other artists.
trivia: Rolling Stone named Waldman the 'singer-songwriter debut of the year' for her 1973 first album.
She was a founding member of the early songwriter collective Bryndle along with Karla Bonoff, Andrew Gold, and Kenny Edwards.
Her father Fred Steiner was a composer who wrote the theme music for Perry Mason and Rocky and Bullwinkle.
youtube_search: Wendy Waldman Love Has Got Me
